title: Recycled paper sheet forming, making new sheets from old
subtitle: How to turn waste paper into usable handmade sheets
excerpt: Printed paper, junk mail, and brown envelopes can all be repulped and formed into new sheets. The recycled sheets are softer and shorter in fibre than cotton-linter sheets, but they produce a distinctive, slightly rough surface well suited to watercolour painting and journalling.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923): Hunter's account of early rag paper formation and the role of recycled material in the tradition. General technique consistent with the centuries-old practice of retting and repulping textile and paper waste that Hunter documents from historic papermaking sites.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of reducing paper back to its individual fibres by soaking and blending, so it can be re-formed into a new sheet.
The evenness of fibre distribution in a sheet of paper, visible when the sheet is held to light. Good formation shows even translucency; poor formation has clumps and gaps.
Transferring the newly formed wet sheet from the mould surface to a flat absorbent surface (a 'couch sheet' or felt) by pressing the mould face-down and peeling it away.

content.text: The fibres in commercial paper have already been processed and shortened.

recycled paper produces a pulp with shorter fibres than cotton linter; the resulting sheets are weaker but still perfectly usable for art and journalling work. Adding a small proportion of cotton linter (roughly 20% by dry weight) improves strength and
markedly without requiring a full cotton-linter batch.

attrs.title: Papermaking blender safety
attrs.body: Keep the blender unplugged when loading paper pieces or rinsing it. Do not reach into the blender jar while it is plugged in. Drain the vat before reaching in with both hands.

items.name: Waste paper
items.notes: Uncoated paper only, photocopier paper, brown envelopes, junk mail printed on plain stock. Coated paper (the glossy type) does not break down well and produces a slick, weak sheet. Remove any sticky tape or staples before soaking.

items.name: Blender
items.notes: A dedicated blender kept for papermaking only. The blade will dull; do not use the kitchen blender for food after it has been used for pulp.

items.name: Papermaking vat
items.notes: Large enough to submerge the A4 mould fully.

items.name: Cotton linter (optional)
items.notes: 20% by weight mixed into the recycled pulp improves sheet strength.

items.name: Calcium carbonate (whiting) (optional)
items.notes: A small amount added to the vat buffers against the acidity of some recycled papers.

items.name: Felts or absorbent cloths
items.notes: For couching the wet sheets. Old wool blanket cut into A4-sized squares works well.

content.text: Tear the waste paper into small pieces (roughly 3 × 3 cm) and soak in clean water for at least 30 minutes, preferably 2-3 hours. The soaked pieces should feel soft and slightly slimy. Load the blender half full with water, add a small handful of soaked paper (roughly 15-20 grams dry weight), and blend for thirty seconds on high speed. The pulp should be a smooth, cloudy slurry with no visible paper fragments. Blend in two or three small batches rather than one large one.
Fill the vat and pull the sheet

Pour the blended pulp into the vat and fill with clean water to a depth of 200-250 mm. Stir vigorously with both hands. Hold the mould with the deckle seated firmly on top. Dip the mould-and-deckle assembly into the vat at a forward angle, then level it out in one smooth movement as it passes through the pulp. Lift the mould slowly and straight up from the vat, tilting causes uneven
. Shake gently in a small figure-of-eight motion to even out the fibres. Hold the mould level until most of the water has drained.

transfers the wet sheet from the mould to the felt. Remove the deckle. Turn the mould over onto a damp felt in one smooth motion, pressing the mesh face against the felt. Roll a sponge or the back of a wide brush firmly across the back of the mould to press the sheet from the mesh onto the felt. Lift the mould slowly from one edge; the sheet should transfer cleanly. Cover with a second felt and press firmly with both hands to remove as much water as possible before moving the sheet to dry.
